R,vi MEHT.-\
CHR’.
FAIR
POLITICAL PRACTICES CoMMIssION
June 6,
1995
Deborah Borba
Kings County Water District
200 North Campus Drive
Hanford,
CA
93230
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-l65
Dear Ms.
Borba:
You have requested advice on behalf of the Kings County Water
District regarding public officials’ filing obligations under the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).’!
QUESTIONS
1.
Are the Board of Directors and District Manager “other
public officials who manage public investments” under Section
87200 and should they file a Form 721, rather than a Form 730?
2.
Are the trustees of the employee money purchase plan
“other public officials who manage public investments” under
Section 87200 and should they file a Form 721, rather than a Form
730?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
The Board of Directors and the District Manager are
“other public officials who manage public investments” and should
file a Form 721.
2.
The trustees of the employees’ money purchase plan may be
“other public officials who manage public investments” if they
exercise responsibility for the management of the employees’ plan.
If they do, they are “other public officials who manage public
investments” and should file a Form 721.
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
s:.
N()). S;
•
2S6t)


File No.
A—95--165
Page
2
FACTS
The Kings County Water District (“District”)
is a special
district in the county providing water stock rental for irrigation
to farmers and ground water recharge services.
The District’s
sources of revenue include tax revenues, user charges, fees and
interest income.
The District funds are placed in three locations.
Approximately $1.5 million is with the State Treasurer’s Local
Agency Investment Fund
(“LAIF”)
and the Kings County Auditor-
Controller,
and approximately $60,000 is with a local bank.
The
District Manager and Board of Directors review receipts,
expenditures, revenues and anticipated cash flow.
The District
Manager and Board of Directors determine whether funds should be
transferred to either the LAIF or the county, or remain at the
local bank.
In addition, the District has a money purchase plan available
to its employees.
The District contributes
10 percent of the
employees’ salaries to this plan.
Two of the Directors and two of
the employees are trustees of this account.
2
!
ANALYSIS
Section 87200 of the Act lists those officials,
including
“other public officials who manage public investments,” who are
subject to the disclosure requirements of Article 2,
Chapter
7 of
the Act,
and must file full disclosure of their economic interests
on Form 721.
Other public officials or employees who are
designated in their agency’s conflict—of-interest code are subject
to the disclosure requirements of Article 3,
Chapter
7 of the Act
and must file Form 730.
These individuals’ disclosure of economic
interests may range from partial to full,
as provided in their
agency’s code.
Regulation 18720(a)
sets forth the following definitions to
assist in interpreting the phrase “other public officials who
manage public investments”:
(1)
“Public investments” means the investment of
public moneys in real estate, securities, or other
economic interests for the production of revenue or
other financial return.
2/
In your letter you indicated that Principal Financial Group is
the plan sponsor.
You are not seeking advice concerning their
participation,
however, the persons within Principal Financial
Group who make investment decisions may be “public officials who
manage public investments.”


File No.
A—95—165
Page
3
(2)
“Public moneys” means all moneys belonging to,
received by, or held by, the state,
or any city,
county,
town, district,
or public agency therein,
or by an
officer thereof acting in his official capacity,
and
includes the proceeds of all bonds and other evidence of
indebtedness, trust funds held by public pension and
retirement systems, deferred compensation funds held for
investments by public agencies,
and public moneys held
by a financial institution under a trust indenture to
which a public agency is a party.
(3)
“Management of public investments” means the
following non-ministerial functions:
directing the
investment of public moneys; formulating or approving
investment policies; approving or establishing
guidelines for asset allocations;
or approving
investment transactions.”
Regulation 18720(b)
defines “other public officials who
manage public investments” to include:
(1)
Members of boards and commissions,
including
pension and retirement boards or commissions,
or of
committees thereof,
who exercise responsibility for the
management of public investments;
[and)
(2)
High—level officers and employees of public
agencies who exercise primary responsibility for the
management of public investments,
such as chief or
principal investment officers or chief financial
managers.
This category shall not include officers and
employees who work under the supervision of the chief or
principal investment officers or the chief financial
managers;
and
(3)
Individuals who, pursuant to a contract with a
state or local government agency, perform the same or
substantially all of the same functions that would
otherwise be performed by the public officials described
in subdivision
(b) (2)
above.
***
The question of who is considered a “public official who
manages public investments” under Section 87200 and Regulation
18720 is a fact—based one, which depends on how each agency
operates.
In many agencies, the entire board may have the
authority to direct or approve investment policies or
transactions.
In others, this function may be performed by an
executive committee of the board in charge of investments.
In
still others, the treasurer, the chief financial manager,
an
accountant,
or other individuals,
may be the ones who are solely
or primarily in charge of the agency’s investment decisions and
policy.
Therefore,
determinations about whether board members,


File No.
A—95—165
Page
4
executive committee members, treasurers,
or other individuals,
should file Statements of Economic Interests on Form 721 as a
“public official who manages public investments,” versus filing on
form 730 as a designated employee, will necessarily vary,
depending on the duties of these individuals.
The Kings County Water District funds constitute public
moneys under the definition of Regulation 18720(a)
above.
You
stated that the District Manager and Board of Directors are the
officials responsible for determining whether funds get
transferred to either the LAIF,
the County or to a local bank.
Accordingly, the District Manager and the Board of Directors are
“public officials who manage public investments” within the
meaning of Section 87200,
and accordingly,
should file Form 721,
rather than Form 730.
You did not indicate the role the trustees play in the
management of the employees’ money purchase plan.
If those
trustees,
or if any individual within Principal Financial Group
exercise responsibility for the management of the employees’ plan,
they, too,
are “public officials who manage public investments”
within the meaning of Section 87200,
and should file Form 721,
rather than Form 730./
Since the Kings County Water District is a single-county
district, it should work with the Kings County Counsel to amend
its conflict of interest code to remove the Board Members,
District Manager and Trustees from the code.
These persons need
not re-file Form 721 at this time,
but can wait until the next
annual filing deadline and file Form 721 at that time.
Remember,
however, that any person who assumes or leaves office between now
and the annual filing deadline,
should file their respective
assuming or leaving office statement on the Form 721.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact the Cornanission’s Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
I
Jeanette E. Iturvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
Since we have already determined that the board of directors
are “public officials who manage public investments,” those
directors who also serve as trustees of the money purchase plan
are not required to file a separate Form 721 as a trustee.
General

exercise responsibility for the management of the employees’ plan.
If they do, they are “other public officials who manage public
investments” and should file a Form 721.Commission regulations appear at

Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

QUESTION: 1.
Are the Board of Directors and District Manager “other
public officials who manage public investments” under Section
87200 and should they file a Form 721, rather than a Form 730?
2.
Are the trustees of the employee money purchase plan
“other public officials who manage public investments” under
Section 87200 and should they file a Form 721, rather than a Form
730?

CONCLUSION: 1.
The Board of Directors and the District Manager are
“other public officials who manage public investments” and should
file a Form 721.
2.
The trustees of the employees’ money purchase plan may be
“other public officials who manage public investments” if they
exercise responsibility for the management of the employees’ plan.
If they do, they are “other public officials who manage public
investments” and should file a Form 721.Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
